args.query: site:irs.gov Pub 515 nonresident alien Form W-4 instructions employee

content.content:
content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Federal income tax reporting and withholding on wages paid to aliens

  - url: https://www.irs.gov/individuals/international-taxpayers/federal-income-tax-reporting-and-withholding-on-wages-paid-to-aliens

  - source: Internal Revenue Service

  - domain: irs.gov


  ## Content


  # Federal income tax reporting and withholding on wages paid to aliens


  Employers that hire workers who are not U.S. citizens must first determine which
  federal tax-residency category applies for withholding purposes. The IRS describes
  a general process in which workers are separated into resident aliens and nonresident
  aliens under federal tax rules or an applicable tax treaty.


  For federal wage withholding, resident aliens are generally treated in the same
  manner as U.S. citizens. Nonresident aliens are subject to a separate set of withholding
  rules. Those rules are described further in IRS employer publications on wage withholding
  and tax treatment of nonresident aliens.


  ## Form W-4 treatment for nonresident alien employees


  A nonresident alien employee completing Form W-4 must follow the supplemental instructions
  in Notice 1392. The IRS also directs nonresident alien employees to use the related
  withholding guidance for their status rather than relying only on the ordinary employee
  instructions.


  The IRS guidance includes several special Form W-4 rules for employees who are within
  the nonresident-alien withholding category. Among other requirements, the employee
  identifies the nonresident-alien status on the form, cannot claim exemption from
  withholding in the ordinary way, and follows special limitations and instructions
  for certain Form W-4 entries.


  The IRS also explains that employers use a special procedure when calculating federal
  income tax withholding on wages paid to nonresident alien employees. The employer-side
  calculation rules are described in Publication 15 and Publication 15-T.


  ## Treaty-based and other exceptions


  Some nonresident aliens may qualify for a federal income tax withholding exemption
  on wages under an income tax treaty. The IRS directs those employees to the separate
  Form 8233 process for treaty-based claims. A payroll office that accepts Form 8233
  has additional review and forwarding duties.


  The page also describes limited statutory exceptions that can apply to particular
  short-term services or certain individuals temporarily present in specific nonimmigrant
  categories when all listed conditions are met. One listed exception concerns a short
  stay, a compensation ceiling, and services for certain foreign employers or foreign
  offices. Another concerns individuals in specified F, J, M, or Q status when compensation
  is paid by one of the foreign payers described by the IRS. These exceptions are
  separate from the ordinary employee path and do not make any one withholding process
  universal for all employees.


  When a treaty exemption is claimed through Form 8233, the payroll withholding agent
  has review responsibilities and, after acceptance, forwards the form to the IRS
  within the period stated on the page. The IRS also distinguishes treaty-exempt wages
  reported on Form 1042-S from additional wages that may be reported on Form W-2.
  These reporting rules are employer responsibilities and do not by themselves determine
  which Form W-4 instruction set applies to an employee.


  If a nonresident alien does not furnish a proper Form W-4 when required, the employer
  applies the default withholding treatment described in the IRS rules. The page also
  notes that supplemental wage payments may follow different withholding procedures.


  ## Applicability depends on the employee''s tax category


  The central distinction in the IRS process is the employee''s federal tax-residency
  classification for withholding purposes. Resident aliens are generally handled like
  U.S. citizens for withholding. Nonresident aliens are handled under the special
  withholding rules and use the supplemental Form W-4 instructions in Notice 1392.


  The page does not determine any particular employee''s current classification. An
  employer or employee must establish the current case-specific tax category before
  deciding which instruction set governs a Form W-4 update.

  '

content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: About Notice 1392, Supplemental Form W-4 Instructions for Nonresident Aliens

  - url: https://www.irs.gov/forms-pubs/about-notice-1392

  - source: Internal Revenue Service

  - domain: irs.gov


  ## Content


  # About Notice 1392, Supplemental Form W-4 Instructions for Nonresident Aliens


  Notice 1392 is an IRS supplemental instruction document for nonresident aliens who
  complete Form W-4 for compensation paid to them as employees performing dependent
  personal services in the United States.


  The page identifies Notice 1392 as a special set of instructions connected to Form
  W-4. Its scope is not the general population of employees. It is tied to the nonresident-alien
  employee category described by the IRS.


  ## Current revision


  The IRS page links to the current revision of Notice 1392. The notice is intended
  to be used together with Form W-4 when the employee falls within the nonresident-alien
  scope covered by the supplemental instructions.


  ## Related IRS materials


  The IRS page also links to related federal tax materials, including:


  - Publication 15, the Employer''s Tax Guide;

  - Publication 519, the U.S. Tax Guide for Aliens;

  - Publication 901, U.S. Tax Treaties;

  - Form 8233, which is used for certain withholding-exemption claims involving compensation
  paid to nonresident aliens; and

  - Form W-4, Employee''s Withholding Certificate.


  These related materials address different parts of the federal tax and withholding
  framework. The presence of Notice 1392 does not itself establish that any particular
  employee is currently a nonresident alien for withholding purposes. The employee''s
  current case must first fall within the notice''s stated scope before the supplemental
  instructions govern the Form W-4 process.


  The IRS page reports no current recent developments for Notice 1392. It serves primarily
  as the official index page for the notice and its related products.

  '

content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Topic no. 753, Form W-4, Employees Withholding Certificate

  - url: https://www.irs.gov/taxtopics/tc753

  - source: Internal Revenue Service

  - domain: irs.gov


  ## Content


  # Topic no. 753, Form W-4, Employees Withholding Certificate


  ## General information


  Form W-4 provides an employer with information used to calculate federal income
  tax withholding from an employee''s pay. It includes the employee''s filing status
  and may include adjustments for multiple jobs, credits, other income, deductions,
  and any additional amount the employee wants withheld.


  When an employee gives an employer a revised Form W-4, the employer generally must
  put the revision into effect within the timing rules described by the IRS. The employer
  must follow the revised certificate unless an invalid Form W-4 or an IRS lock-in
  letter changes what the employer is permitted to use.


  The IRS permits employers to receive Forms W-4 through an electronic system that
  satisfies the applicable federal rules. Employees may use the current official form
  or an employer substitute that meets IRS requirements. An employee-created substitute
  does not qualify simply because it contains similar information.


  ## Special instructions are not universal


  The ordinary Form W-4 instructions are not the only instruction set mentioned in
  the IRS guidance. The IRS specifically states that nonresident aliens must follow
  special instructions when completing Form W-4. Employers are directed to Notice
  1392 and other IRS guidance for employees in that category.


  The topic therefore distinguishes the general employee process from a conditionally
  applicable special process. The special instructions depend on the employee being
  within the relevant nonresident-alien withholding category; they are not described
  as rules for every employee.


  For employer calculations involving wages of nonresident alien employees, the IRS
  points to Publication 15-T. This calculation guidance is separate from the employee''s
  task of determining which Form W-4 instruction set applies.


  ## Exemption from withholding


  An employee may use Form W-4 to claim exemption from federal income tax withholding
  only when the IRS eligibility conditions are satisfied. A claim of exemption generally
  applies only for the calendar year in which it is furnished. Continued exemption
  in a later year requires a new certificate by the applicable deadline.


  If an employee does not provide a renewed exemption certificate when required, the
  employer uses the default withholding treatment described by the IRS. A later certificate
  can affect future wages but does not automatically refund amounts already withheld.


  ## Invalid certificates and recordkeeping


  Unauthorized changes or additions can make a Form W-4 invalid. When an employer
  receives an invalid certificate, the employer does not use it to determine withholding
  and may continue using an earlier valid certificate when the IRS rules permit.


  Employers must keep completed Forms W-4 in their records for the required retention
  period. Electronic systems must also be able to produce a hard copy when needed.


  ## Lock-in letters


  In some cases, the IRS can issue a lock-in letter that limits the withholding settings
  an employer may accept for a particular employee. After the lock-in arrangement
  takes effect, the employer must disregard a later Form W-4 that would reduce withholding
  below the permitted level, while still honoring a certificate that increases withholding.


  These lock-in rules are another example of a conditional constraint. They do not
  replace the need to determine which general or special Form W-4 instructions apply
  to the employee''s current case.

  '
metadata: {}
title: Topic no. 753, Form W-4, Employees Withholding Certificate
url: https://www.irs.gov/taxtopics/tc753

Your current case is recorded as:

- **Federal tax classification:** Nonresident alien  
- **Status:** Active and currently applied to federal wage withholding  
- **Effective date:** January 15, 2026

Therefore, you should follow the **nonresident-alien Form W-4 process**, not only the standard employee instructions.

### What to follow

1. **Complete the current Form W-4** through your employer’s payroll system or using the official IRS form.
2. **Use the supplemental instructions in IRS Notice 1392** for nonresident aliens. These instructions govern special W-4 requirements and limitations for your classification.
3. **Do not claim ordinary “exempt from withholding” status** unless you meet the specific IRS requirements for your situation.
4. If you are claiming a **tax-treaty exemption for compensation**, that is generally a separate **Form 8233** process—not simply a W-4 entry. Confirm with your payroll or international-tax office whether they accept and require Form 8233.
5. Submit the completed certificate through your employer’s designated process. Payroll will apply the special nonresident-alien withholding calculation.

Official IRS resources:

- [Notice 1392 — Supplemental Form W-4 Instructions for Nonresident Aliens](https://www.irs.gov/forms-pubs/about-notice-1392)
- [Federal income tax reporting and withholding on wages paid to aliens](https://www.irs.gov/individuals/international-taxpayers/federal-income-tax-reporting-and-withholding-on-wages-paid-to-aliens)
- [IRS Topic No. 753 — Form W-4](https://www.irs.gov/taxtopics/tc753)

This identifies the applicable process; the correct entries on your W-4 still depend on your personal circumstances, such as other jobs, dependents, deductions, additional withholding, and possible treaty eligibility.

- **Federal tax classification:** Nonresident alien  
- **Status:** Active and currently applied to federal wage withholding  
- **Effective date:** January 15, 2026

Therefore, you should follow the **nonresident-alien Form W-4 process**, not only the standard employee instructions.

### What to follow

1. **Complete the current Form W-4** through your employer’s payroll system or using the official IRS form.
2. **Use the supplemental instructions in IRS Notice 1392** for nonresident aliens. These instructions govern special W-4 requirements and limitations for your classification.
3. **Do not claim ordinary “exempt from withholding” status** unless you meet the specific IRS requirements for your situation.
4. If you are claiming a **tax-treaty exemption for compensation**, that is generally a separate **Form 8233** process—not simply a W-4 entry. Confirm with your payroll or international-tax office whether they accept and require Form 8233.
5. Submit the completed certificate through your employer’s designated process. Payroll will apply the special nonresident-alien withholding calculation.

Official IRS resources:

- [Notice 1392 — Supplemental Form W-4 Instructions for Nonresident Aliens](https://www.irs.gov/forms-pubs/about-notice-1392)
